stem:
A 30-year-old G2P1 woman at 39+3 weeks gestation has been admitted in established labour, cervix 5 cm dilated with intact membranes. Her vaginal-rectal swab at 36+0 weeks grew group B Streptococcus. She has no documented penicillin allergy and tolerated amoxicillin for a UTI earlier in this pregnancy. Her previous baby was born vaginally at term and did not develop neonatal sepsis. The intrapartum CTG is reactive with normal variability and accelerations.

The midwife is asking for prescription of intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 82/min, blood pressure 118/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8°C, foetal heart 140/min reactive
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm dilated, membranes intact, vertex presentation; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: 36 week vaginal-rectal swab: GBS positive; current bloods unremarkable; CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily commenced now and continued for 48 hours postpartum.
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve the rapid amniotic fluid levels required for neonatal GBS prophylaxis; IV therapy is essential. (Source: RANZCOG GBS statement)

options.text: IV vancomycin 20 mg/kg over 1 hour given every 8 hours throughout labour until delivery.
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with confirmed clindamycin resistance, not routine first-line prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG GBS statement)

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g loading dose then 1 g every 8 hours until delivery as first-line therapy.
options.rationale: Cefazolin is the appropriate choice for non-severe penicillin allergy without anaphylaxis history, not first-line over benzylpenicillin. (Source: RANZCOG GBS statement)

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 3 g intravenously as a loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery.
options.rationale: RANZCOG and AMH recommend intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours as first-line for GBS-positive women without penicillin allergy. (Source: RANZCOG GBS statement)

options.text: Defer antibiotics until rupture of membranes occurs because she is currently low risk for transmission.
options.rationale: Antibiotics should be commenced once labour is established to achieve adequate amniotic fluid penetration before delivery. (Source: RANZCOG GBS statement)

explanation.summary: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for a known GBS-positive woman in established labour is IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose followed by 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery. The aim is to achieve at least one full dose at least 4 hours before delivery to reduce neonatal early-onset GBS disease. For mild penicillin allergy (rash, no anaphylaxis), cefazolin 2 g IV load then 1 g 8-hourly is the alternative; for severe allergy, clindamycin 600 mg 8-hourly if susceptibility confirmed, else vancomycin.
key_points: First-line: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g every 4 hours.
Aim for at least one dose at least 4 hours before delivery.
Cefazolin for mild penicillin allergy; clindamycin or vancomycin for severe.
explanation.pearls: Start antibiotics as soon as labour is established (cervix at least 4 cm and regular contractions, or rupture of membranes), not when membranes rupture, to maximise the chance of a 4-hour cover before birth.

stem:
A 34-year-old G3P3 woman is 25 minutes postpartum following a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39 weeks with active third stage. Estimated blood loss is now 1100 mL and ongoing from the placental bed; the placenta is complete on inspection. She was treated for gestational hypertension during pregnancy with labetalol 200 mg twice daily and her blood pressure on the labour ward 30 minutes before delivery was 158/108 mmHg. Bimanual compression has been started and a second 16 G IV cannula is in. Oxytocin 10 IU IM has been given as part of active third stage and an oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1000 mL Hartmann's at 250 mL/hr has been running for 15 minutes.

The consultant is asking what to add next.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 122/min, blood pressure 156/104 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy at the umbilicus, blood loss from below ongoing; perineum intact; bladder catheterised and empty
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L (115-165); platelets 142 x10^9/L; INR 1.1; fibrinogen 2.6 g/L; cross match in progress
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg bd; oxytocin infusion running

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological agent?

options.text: Ergometrine 250 micrograms intramuscularly together with tranexamic acid 1 g intravenously.
options.rationale: Ergometrine causes vasoconstriction and is contraindicated with uncontrolled hypertension, pre-eclampsia, or eclampsia. (Source: RANZCOG PPH guideline)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 micrograms sublingually as a single dose with no additional intravenous therapy.
options.rationale: Misoprostol monotherapy is inadequate at this volume of blood loss when other uterotonic options remain available. (Source: RANZCOG PPH guideline)

options.text: Carbetocin 100 micrograms intravenously as a single bolus dose to replace ongoing oxytocin therapy.
options.rationale: Carbetocin is used at delivery for active management; once oxytocin infusion has failed, the next step is to add a different uterotonic. (Source: RANZCOG PPH guideline)

options.text: Recombinant activated factor VII 90 micrograms/kg intravenously before any further uterotonic doses.
options.rationale: Recombinant factor VIIa is reserved for major haemorrhage refractory to other measures with documented coagulopathy. (Source: RANZCOG PPH guideline)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g intravenously over 10 minutes plus carboprost 250 micrograms intramuscularly.
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated with active hypertension; tranexamic acid plus carboprost are appropriate next-line agents in PPH. (Source: RANZCOG PPH guideline)

explanation.summary: Postpartum haemorrhage management follows a parallel mechanical, pharmacological, and resuscitation pathway. Pharmacological escalation starts with oxytocin, but the second uterotonic depends on contraindications: ergometrine is contraindicated with hypertension, pre-eclampsia, or eclampsia, because it causes intense vasoconstriction. In this woman, the next pharmacological steps are tranexamic acid 1 g IV (ideally within 3 hours of onset, as per the WOMAN trial), and carboprost (15-methyl prostaglandin F2-alpha) 250 micrograms IM, repeatable every 15 minutes up to 8 doses, avoiding it in asthma.
key_points: Ergometrine is contraindicated with hypertension, pre-eclampsia, or eclampsia.
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces mortality (WOMAN trial).
Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma due to bronchospasm risk.
explanation.pearls: When the woman has both hypertension and asthma, the next-line uterotonic is misoprostol 800 micrograms rectal or sublingual alongside tranexamic acid, because both ergometrine and carboprost are off the menu.

stem:
A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 34+5 weeks gestation has been admitted with severe headache, visual scotomata, and right upper quadrant pain. Her booking blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg and BMI 24. Two readings 15 minutes apart in triage have been 168/112 mmHg and 162/110 mmHg. Antihypertensive therapy has been commenced with oral labetalol 200 mg and her blood pressure 30 minutes later is 152/96 mmHg. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, normal foetal movements, and a reactive CTG. The on-call obstetrician has decided to deliver in the next 24 hours after a course of betamethasone.

The team is now discussing seizure prophylaxis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 96/min, blood pressure 152/96 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C, foetal heart 145/min reactive
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes with 2 beats of clonus bilaterally; mild epigastric tenderness; no focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 96 x10^9/L; ALT 72 U/L; creatinine 78 micromol/L; urate 0.42 mmol/L; LDH 320 U/L; PCR 320 mg/mmol
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg orally given; betamethasone 11.4 mg IM started

lead_in: What is the most appropriate magnesium sulfate regimen?

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes then a maintenance infusion of 1 g/hour.
options.rationale: Australian and Magpie trial protocol for severe pre-eclampsia seizure prophylaxis is 4 g IV loading dose then 1 g per hour maintenance. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 6 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by a 2 g per hour maintenance infusion.
options.rationale: Higher dosing regimens were used historically but are not the current Australian standard, which is 4 g and 1 g/hr. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Phenytoin 18 mg/kg intravenous loading dose given over 30 minutes for eclampsia seizure prevention.
options.rationale: Magnesium is superior to phenytoin for both prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures and is the standard of care. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Oral nifedipine 30 mg modified release alone, deferring magnesium until a seizure actually occurs.
options.rationale: In severe pre-eclampsia with neurological features and biochemical derangement, prophylactic magnesium is recommended rather than waiting. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg intravenously as a single dose with onward titration of labetalol infusion only.
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are second-line for refractory eclampsia, not standard prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurological features (headache, visual disturbance), epigastric pain, hypertension at 160/110 mmHg or above, or biochemical derangement (platelets below 100, ALT raised, urate above 0.4 mmol/L) warrants magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis. The Australian SOMANZ regimen, based on the Magpie trial, is a 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g per hour maintenance infusion, continued until 24 hours after delivery or after the last seizure. Monitor patellar reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output; serum magnesium levels are not routinely required if clinical monitoring is reliable.
key_points: SOMANZ regimen: 4 g IV load over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours postpartum.
Monitor patellar reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output for toxicity.
Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10 percent IV is the antidote for magnesium toxicity.
explanation.pearls: Reduce the maintenance to 0.5 g/hour if the creatinine is above 100 micromol/L or urine output falls below 100 mL over 4 hours, because magnesium clearance is renal.

stem:
A 29-year-old G2P0 woman presents to early pregnancy assessment at 7+3 weeks from her last menstrual period with 3 days of mild left iliac fossa pain and minor brown vaginal spotting. She is haemodynamically stable. Her last serum beta-hCG taken at the GP 48 hours ago was 2200 IU/L and today it is 2850 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterine cavity with thin endometrium, a 22 mm left adnexal mass with no foetal pole and no cardiac activity, and a small volume of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Her FBC, renal function, and liver function are within normal limits. She has no significant past history, takes no medications, and lives nearby with reliable transport.

She is keen to avoid surgery if possible and has been counselled about the options.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 80/min, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, mild left iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism, no cervical motion tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 48 hour rise 2200 to 2850 IU/L; TVUS left adnexal 22 mm mass, no FH; small free fluid; Hb 132 g/L; LFTs and creatinine normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG every 48 hours until the value has spontaneously declined.
options.rationale: Expectant management is generally reserved for beta-hCG below 1500 with a clearly declining trend, not the rising value seen here. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Single dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 with follow-up beta-hCG on day 4 and day 7.
options.rationale: She meets RANZCOG methotrexate criteria with beta-hCG under 5000, mass under 3.5 cm, no foetal cardiac activity, stable, and reliable follow-up. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with planned salpingectomy of the affected fallopian tube during the same procedure.
options.rationale: Surgery is appropriate but is not required when methotrexate criteria are met and the patient prefers medical management. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Single dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 plus prophylactic folate 5 mg orally daily for 7 days.
options.rationale: Folate antagonises methotrexate efficacy and should be avoided around treatment. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Emergency theatre for salpingectomy on the basis that any free fluid signals a tubal rupture.
options.rationale: A small volume of free fluid alone in a haemodynamically stable woman is not an indication for emergency surgery. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG criteria for methotrexate in tubal ectopic pregnancy are: haemodynamic stability, beta-hCG below 5000 IU/L, no foetal cardiac activity, ectopic mass below 3.5 cm, normal renal and hepatic function, no contraindications (immunosuppression, blood dyscrasia, breastfeeding, hypersensitivity), reliable follow-up, and patient preference. The dose is 50 mg/m2 IM as a single agent. Follow-up beta-hCG is taken on day 4 and day 7; the day 4 to day 7 fall of at least 15% predicts success, otherwise a second dose is considered.
key_points: RANZCOG methotrexate threshold for beta-hCG is below 5000 IU/L, not below 3000.
Day 4 to day 7 beta-hCG must fall by at least 15 percent for success.
Avoid folate, NSAIDs, alcohol, and conception for 3 months after methotrexate.
explanation.pearls: Surgical management is mandated by RANZCOG when beta-hCG is at or above 5000 IU/L, the mass is at or above 3.5 cm, there is foetal cardiac activity, significant pain, or haemodynamic instability.

stem:
A 27-year-old G1P0 woman with a monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancy at 22+5 weeks gestation is reviewed at the foetal medicine unit after an outside scan reported amniotic fluid discordance. She has felt well; no antepartum bleeding or contractions. The current detailed scan with paired Doppler studies has just been reported by the maternal-foetal medicine consultant. Both foetuses have normal anatomy. Twin A (the donor) is structurally normal with deepest vertical pocket 1.4 cm; twin B (the recipient) has deepest vertical pocket 9.2 cm with a visible bladder.

Umbilical artery Dopplers and ductus venosus traces are also reported.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 76/min, blood pressure 116/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, fundal height 28 cm; cervix on TVUS 36 mm and closed
data_table.Investigations: Twin A EFW 380 g; bladder not visualised; DVP 1.4 cm; UA Doppler with absent end-diastolic flow. Twin B EFW 540 g; visible bladder; DVP 9.2 cm; UA Doppler normal; ductus venosus normal a-wave

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Amnioreduction from the recipient sac alone every 7 days until 28 weeks gestation is reached.
options.rationale: Foetoscopic laser is superior to serial amnioreduction for stage II and above TTTS between 16 and 26 weeks. (Source: NICE multiple pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Expectant management with weekly Dopplers because absent end diastolic flow alone can be transient.
options.rationale: Absent end-diastolic flow in TTTS is Quintero stage III and is not transient; expectant management risks intrauterine death. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Refer urgently for foetoscopic laser photocoagulation of the placental vascular anastomoses.
options.rationale: TTTS Quintero stage III (donor absent end-diastolic flow) at 22+5 weeks meets criteria for foetoscopic laser within 16 to 26 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Selective foeticide of the donor twin by bipolar cord coagulation to maximise recipient survival.
options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for severe foetal anomaly or stage IV TTTS with one twin already moribund. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscularly and plan delivery within 48 hours by caesarean.
options.rationale: Delivery at 22+5 weeks is below the threshold of viability and would not address the underlying anastomotic pathology. (Source: RANZCOG multiple pregnancy guideline)

explanation.summary: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome is diagnosed when monochorionic twins show oligohydramnios with deepest vertical pocket below 2 cm in one sac and polyhydramnios with DVP above 8 cm in the other (Quintero stage I). Staging progresses with non-visible donor bladder (II), abnormal Doppler in either twin including donor absent or reversed end-diastolic umbilical artery flow (III), hydrops (IV), and demise (V). For stage II to IV between 16 and 26 weeks, foetoscopic laser photocoagulation of the inter-twin anastomoses is the standard of care, improving survival of at least one twin to around 80 to 90% in expert centres.
key_points: TTTS staging: I oligo plus poly, II absent donor bladder, III abnormal Doppler, IV hydrops, V demise.
Foetoscopic laser is first-line for Quintero stage II to IV between 16 and 26 weeks.
Amnioreduction is now reserved for centres without laser access or for stage I progression.
explanation.pearls: After laser, surveillance for twin anaemia-polycythaemia sequence (TAPS) with weekly middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity Dopplers is mandatory because residual anastomoses can persist.
